text: Leadership rolled out a values poster about inclusion the same month they let go three of the most senior people of color in the department.
A colleague was passed over for a stretch project after raising a concern about how the team handles diversity in hiring; it felt like retaliation more than coincidence.
Our affinity group ran a strong event, but the participation from executives stayed at the photo-op level.
I watched a manager publicly take credit for the work of two women on our team, and no one corrected the record. For context.
There were both moments of progress and moments where the same old patterns repeated; the gap between stated values and lived experience is real.
Leadership rolled out a values poster about inclusion the same month they let go three of the most senior people of color in the department.
I watched a manager publicly take credit for the work of two women on our team, and no one corrected the record.
When we adjusted our parental leave to be more equitable across roles, it was framed as a fairness issue, not a perk.
When I asked about how promotion criteria are applied, I got vague answers and a sense that I should stop asking.
